data.title: Strawberry Fields: Bought At $9.8/Share, Upside Potential Was Here
data.description:
I've mentioned in a few of my past articles that I recently in 2024-2025 left behind much of my US-based allocation due to correctly proven assumptions of FX overvaluation, thus avoiding a downturn. As things stand now, with FX for USD/SEK and USD/EUR reduced, NA is becoming more attractive for investing again. However, there are companies that were exceptions to my rule of not investing in the US at the time, and one of those exceptions, albeit a small one, is one I'm going to look at today. I will show you why Strawberry Fields REIT (NYSE:STRW) is a company you'll want to keep an eye on - much like I've kept an eye on it in 2025.

As I mentioned in the article found here, I actually have a position in the REIT, but as of my latest update, I considered it a "HOLD" with a sub-$10/share PT. That assumption/rating has outperformed since that time, avoiding a decline of about 3-5%, with the S&P500 outperforming by about 12%.

And while I am going to be raising some excellent, positive points for the company here, I want to remind you what Strawberry Fields, in the end, is. It's a small REIT in a narrow industry and while well-managed, there are much bigger companies with safer fundamentals out there. This is why my position in the company, despite the positivity I'm about to share, is small.

Let's see what we have for STRW as of 1H25, and going into the next few years.

Strawberry Fields REIT - long-term potential upside is here, and outperformance is possible.

My last article on the company is roughly 3 months old at this time. My reason for updating at this time is really only one - I recently bought more Strawberry Fields, because the company for a short time, met my investment criteria and my PT of below $10/share. For a short time a few weeks back in May of 2025, it traded at below $10/share, setting off an alert at my broker.

Now, because, the USD/SEK FX pairing at the time was below 9.5 SEK per USD compared to over 11 SEK at it's high, I managed to scoop up shares at $9.8 per share.

Why did I do this?

First off, STRW at that price yielded over 5.1% - which for this REIT is excellent. Even with a market capitalization of less than $150M, this is a company I like. The uplisting from OTC to NYSE was a big reason for me to increase me interest back in -24 (uplisting in -23), but rather, this is a company that's been grown from the "ground up" by current management, and done so extremely skillfully, judging by the way that the CEO has managed to generate AFFO and growth over a period of 2 decades.

As I mentioned in my previous article, the company's CEO started buying up skilled nursing facilities, which now number in excess of 130 in 11 states around the US. Strawberry Fields has a very strong experience of this type of triple-net leasing, and unlike other REITs in the field, tenant concentration is not that big a deal.

The company generates AFFO in excess of $16M per quarter, confirmed in the recently-reported 1Q25 period, with almost $17M of AFFO, or over $60M of AFFO per year. In terms of leverage or debt, which is the keystone to any REIT's success, the company's debt is all fixed, low-interest, and long-term HUD guaranteed. The average interest rate for the company is 3.91%, which is comparable to European companies in the same sector, but European companies have about 40-150x the market cap of this company. The company's low interest rate is part of what In my view, makes STRW so interesting. Furthermore, this debt has a maturity of over 20 years.

You may not get 100-200% RoR in the near term - I don't see STRW managing this - but you will be flying under the radar with a very appealing sort of exposure, while earning about 5% yield on your invested capital in. dividends alone.

So, leverage and debt remain a "check" for 1Q25. Nothing has changed in terms of risk. The company's track record of growth has also remained steady, with adjusted EBITDA and FFO growth in the double digits, for FFO, almost 14% CAGR.

Strawberry Fields, and its management under CEO Mr. Moishe Gubin, has shown itself to be extremely risk-averse. The company's slow growth over time, it's 20+ year history prior to listing, its sub-52% net leverage ratio, and also its 100% rent collections over time confirm this to me.

The company's tenancy has a mostly geographical profile - meaning that tenants are usually concentrated in geographical areas, which I actually like. Why do I like this?

Because it implies that the company's tenants have a wealth of local experience and efficiency.

The company, as you can see, even states that it's a focus for STRW to acquire geographically concentrated facilities. I believe that the failures or risks of skilled nursing/healthcare operators are well-known to most of the companies in the sector, also emerging ones - and when it comes to STRW, the company seems very insistent not to make similar mistakes (over-leverage, tenant risks) as others.

The company's M&A strategy and underwriting criteria is extremely conservative, even when compared to other companies in the field - but STRW also continues to find assets for purchase, several in 2025 in fact.

The company's focus on off-market smaller deals means that the competition for the types of deals that the company does is very limited - sometimes it's likely that STRW is the only, or one of the only interested parties involved.

While a lot of published negativity has concerned the nursing home industry across the US, with even me having done some poor investments in the sector, the fact is that the moats to this industry are not insignificant. A combination of significant construction costs (due to regulation and knowledge requirement), with a complex funding system with federal and state-level subsidies through the combination of Medicaid and Medicare, has resulted in a very low supply of nursing homes and licensing restrictions. From a personal standpoint, I have experience in the industry as well, though not in the US, as I, on a director level, managed five skilled nursing homes with a total of 165 beds in my time as an external consulting director for a public institution during 2022-2024.

The moats I speak of are not unique to the US - these requirements and hesitancy for new supply are present in Europe/Sweden as well, due to the requirements when constructing these buildings. Even public organizations are very hesitant to build new ones - and this creates a moat for any company that operates them expertly. Despite the differences in our systems, I recognize the value of such an operator. That is in fact why I became interested in the first place - I saw the things the company was doing, and recognized stability that we really didn't see in other, bigger companies.

Strawberry Fields is therefore on a growth trajectory. I believe this growth trajectory to be intact even for the future. As long as the company applies its conservative underwriting and investment approach, I see no reason why the company should do significantly worse or differently than it has to this time. The company is in a position where it's "flying below the radar", so it can comfortably acquire off-market deals using the same logic for years to come, rewarding shareholders with both dividends and stability. At least theoretically.

One of the company's benefits is also debt issuances, which, unlike other US-based companies, are done on the Tel Aviv stock exchange, due to the management background and ties to Israel. I view this as an incredible advantage due to the long maturity times.

On the flip side, as I mentioned, one of the most important things is not to expect outsized growth. It's my firm stance that Strawberry Fields should trade in a very narrow accepted valuation corridor. Any move to the downside of that corridor that is not predicated by worsening of fundamentals is a signal to potentially "BUY" - while any move to the upside, unless the company is seeing significant advantages, is a move to do the opposite - to rotate.

So I keep a close eye on STRW, and as of 1Q25, my valuation thesis is as follows.

Valuation for Strawberry Fields

My case for Strawberry Fields hasn't changed much since my last article. At the time, I gave the company a conservative PT of $10/share based on a fair value of around $13.5-$14.5/share. That's also, as it happens, the corridor of which I spoke.

I expect and forecast an AFFO growth rate of about 9-10% per year, slightly below the company's previous average, to apply a slight discount to growth, and account for the somewhat riskier macro overall. However, I believe that STRW should trade at least at a 9.5x P/AFFO rate to account for the positive qualities and growth that we've seen. At a 9.5x P/AFFO, the company currently has a forward growth/return potential of around 13.5% per year - lower than my hurdle. However, when I bought at $9.8/share, the company's upside to this same multiple was over 16% per year. I consider the company's 9-10x P/AFFO multiple also to be correct, because it represents nearly a 50% multiple discount to the larger players in this industry, reflecting its size risk.

The company's drops to a valuation where the upside is appealing are, as you can see in the trend above, usually very short. The company usually bounces up fairly quickly, as it has with my recent buy, hence why I'm at a decent profit for my position. The best approach one could do for STRW, I believe, to put a price alert below $10/share - as I had the previous time around.

However, due to confirmed AFFO growth in 1Q25, and my expectation for this growth to continue, I am raising my PT to one of $10.5/share - and also raising my alert to that level. Given the company's small size, I'm allowing for quite a bit of valuation and pricing, but this has to do with the positive qualities I mentioned. I can also show you that the company has significantly outperformed the broader market, with much bigger peers, that are also much riskier in terms of operator risk.

Granted, you can't (in my view) say that STRW, a company with a sub-$200M market capitalization, should trade at the 15-16x P/AFFO multiple of say, NHI or Caretrust. That would make no sense, given the size differences. But I would say at a 9-10x P/AFFO valuation, the company's size is not an obstacle to investing in it - not under this management, and not with this operating history. The company has proven, over time, that it does not overuse debt to fund risky acquisitions, in fact, that it does not do risky acquisitions in a way that threatens its survival. It seems very comfortable with a "slow growth", and this is something that I both respect and appreciate in an investment like this.

It's something I can get behind - much like I got behind the investment approach for Agree Realty (ADC) when first investing in the company. I sold off ADC for a combination of FX and valuation reasons back in early 2025 with a very nice profit, but would be thrilled to get back in at a low, appealing multiple.

Strawberry Fields has some of the same appeal as this, $8.3B market cap REIT. and that should not be ignored.

I should also mention, to any Israel-based investor, the company has a native Tel Aviv ticker, STRW, which currently trades at ₪38 - so if you have access to this, there might be tax advantages that I am unaware of.

Risks to my thesis

Risks exist to every company. Because I rate the company as "HOLD", there is both an upside and downside risk. The upside risk is that I am too conservative, and the company could see a sudden increase in valuation. Aside from the size variable, I have no reason or background in saying the company shouldn't be above 10x P/AFFO. It's a straight peer-based assumption, with no real argument beyond this, aside from the fact that this is where the company has been for the past 2 years. I should also mention in terms of upside risk that my PT of $10.5/share is the lower end of the range. 7 Analysts follow STRW, and they have the company at an average of $13.1/share, with 6 out of 7 at a "BUY" recommendation here. I am therefore going against the grain - with my reason being that I believe a small company should be discounted.

On the downside, if the company were to underperform, such as sticking its 2-year current AFFO average of 8.4x, that would in turn spell a return of less than 8% per year, inclusive of dividends. So there's a scenario here where you are certainly not making as much money as in some safer stocks.

But it's a value play - and I don't see value destruction or fundamental danger as a real possibility. So, despite the company size, I view overall risk as low to moderate, and I would argue with anyone saying that this company is "speculative" simply because of its size.

Thesis

  • Strawberry REIT is an attractive skilled nursing REIT under superb management with a great upside at the right sort of valuation - unfortunately, we currently don't have the "right" valuation to make it work, though we had it a few weeks ago when I bought at $9.8/share.
  • I have a small in this somewhat thinly traded REIT, and one I intend to keep unless the company goes above $14/share. If that were to happen, I would then probably rotate my stake - just as I would continue to buy more at below my updated PT of $10.5/share.
  • After the reported 1Q25, I am upgrading my PT to $10.5/share, but due to a valuation higher than this, I cannot change my rating at this time, and continue to say "HOLD".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $10.5/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of June 2025

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - U.S. PCE, GDP, Canadian CPI And Many Speeches From Heads Of Central Banks
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Week in review: Central Bank Rate decisions and ongoing Israel-Iran conflict

Geopolitical tensions remain high as Iran continues launching ballistic missiles toward Israel, prompting retaliatory strikes from Israel on Iranian military and nuclear infrastructure.

While headlines are still arriving by the minute, the market's sensitivity has declined somewhat this week, with the conflict increasingly priced in.

Equity indices corrected earlier but rebounded midweek - now selling off again as traders close positions ahead of the weekend, pricing in renewed geopolitical risk.

What has stirred markets more recently is the possibility of U.S. intervention in the conflict. President Trump has indicated a decision may come within the next two weeks, adding a layer of uncertainty.

For position traders, it's worth noting the elevated risk of price gaps when markets reopen on Sunday evening.

U.S. crude oil is trading higher on the week but has pulled back after a failed breakout attempt near key resistance, closing the week around $75.

On the monetary policy front, major central banks - including the Fed, Bank of England, Swiss National Bank, Bank of Japan, and People's Bank of China - held rate decisions this week.

Only the SNB moved, cutting rates to 0% and signaling that a return to negative territory remains on the table if the Swiss franc strengthens excessively. This reflects growing concern over a slowing domestic economy and weakening exports.

In forex, the U.S. dollar staged a comeback as dollar selling positions became overcrowded, and risk sentiment tilted more cautious. Traders are leaning back into the greenback amid global uncertainty.

The Week ahead: Inflation reports, Central Bank heads scheduled

Keep your expectations for volatility high, with conflict headlines still being released throughout the day in every session and a potential US Intervention that would surely be market moving.

Asia Pacific Markets - Australian CPI and another Inflation data point for Japan

APAC Markets aren't the busiest in the upcoming week, but one thing to look at is a reversal of previous week's gains on currencies such as the AUD and NZD, which had decent performance since the RBA and RBNZ Rate Decisions.

Tuesday evening should see the release of the Australian May Inflation report, expected at 2.4% year-over-year.

Further reports on Japanese inflation for June are also expected on Thursday at 19:30 ET, with the Core data expected at 3.3% y/y.

Economic Data from Europe, UK and North America - US GDP, PCE and Canada CPI

This week also won't be the busiest in terms of data release; however, markets will surely brace for both the US GDP and the Core PCE as markets keep assembling data on the effect of US Tariffs on the world's largest economy.

Core PCE data is expected at 0.1% month-over-month (Friday morning 8:30 ET) and the annualized Q1 GDP figure expected at -0.2% on Thursday at 8:30 A.M.

Canada will also see the release of their own CPI report on Wednesday at 8:30, with the Headline data forecasted at 1.5% and Core CPI expected at 2.6%.

Other than data, many Heads of central banks will be speaking with ECB's Lagarde on Monday speaking at the European Parliament in Brussels, Fed Chair Powell testifying on Wednesday (usually accompanied by a speech on Monetary outlooks), BOE's Bailey talking at the British Chambers of Commerce Global Annual Conference on Thursday.

Fed's Williams will also be speaking on Friday (7:30) in Basel, Switzerland, where we will see his own view on the upcoming Rate Decision after this morning's dovish surprise from Chris Waller.

Also, as always, stay in touch with the latest developments in the Middle East to avoid being on the wrong side of sudden volatility spikes!

For all market-moving economic releases and events, see the MarketPulse Economic Calendar.

US Dollar performance versus other Forex Major counterparts

Have a good weekend, and safe trades for the week ahead!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Humble Group's Growth Belongs To Future Snacking, But The Market Remains Skeptical
data.description:
Introduction

Humble group is a Swedish company that operates within the food industry. They develop and distribute different types of sugar and calorie reduced products. This article is about the ticker Humble Group (OTCPK:HMBAF), but the article is more aimed for the investors who can access the Nasdaq Stockholm where this stock has much higher volume, and goes under the ticker “HUMBLE”. I believe Humble Group holds a lot of potential, but is currently on a negative spiral due to bad performance last quarter. Humble's Group shares are falling, and I don't believe this is the right time to buy it; therefore, I rate this stock a HOLD!

What has happened?

Humble Group financial history has shown signs of strong growth in the past four years. In 2020, they had revenues just slightly over SEK 33 million, and in 2024 they sold products for over SEK 7,700 million, that is quite the growth in only four years. Humble Group has used leverage and IPO capital to acquire new companies that fit their business model and grow even faster, and many of these businesses have seen immense growth in the past years. However, Humble group did not become profitable until 2023, so it is a rather young company with already strong presence in the Scandinavian market.

Humble Group has met some difficulties going into 2025. The main ticker on Nasdaq Stockholm is down more than 30% this year. There could be several reasons why, but the main reason could be investors worried about market share and pause in growth. The CEO mentioned that part of the slowdown comes from Easter.

The late Easter implies that a large proportion of sell-in will take place in April, while we see good conditions to deliver higher organic growth during the remainder of the year.

Humble group has a high multiple valuation due to its strong growth in the past years, but it is seeing pressure right now on its valuation.

Humble Group AB sales were up 4% YoY last quarter, notably was gross profit up 9% which shows Humble Group was able to increase gross margin due to optimize purchasing and shipping more cost-efficient. Profitability was stable around 5% both this quarter and TTM, only if we exclude non items affecting comparability. Humble Group had cost worth of SEK 18 million related to restructuring costs, Lock-in penalty from acquisition SPA and other.

Future snacking was up 19% YoY and reached SEK 282 million in sales last quarter. Future snacking saw strong demand throughout their portfolio, with key demand for confectionary and snacking products. Brands like Pandy and True Co. saw high double-digit growth. I am particularly interested in this segment, and believe the products that they are currently producing are quite innovative. I am a regular customer of their Pandy products, which offers low-calorie candy which tastes very similar to regular candy but 1/3 of the calories. I am paying close attention to this segment going forward, since I believe this segment could witness very strong and stable growth going forward.

Quality nutrition saw sales decline 7% YoY due to customers postponing orders and also a slowdown for their manufacturing contracts services since Humble Group also produces goods for other companies. Lower demand caused lower utilisation which impacted profitability, EBIT margins going from 6.7% to 5.2% YoY.

Sustainable care's net sales increased 4% YoY, and gross margin has increased from 35.6% to 39.3% YoY, where the main cause consumer change towards higher quality and premium brands as Humble Co. and Naty. If this is an overall trend change, then that's a good sign for this segment going into the future.

Nordic Distribution which is their biggest segment saw a net sales increase of 6% YoY, which is good a sign of continued growth, however, gross margin contraction due to inventory sell out and combined with a weaker product mix saw margins fall for Nordic Distribution this quarter, and down more than 220 b.p. Yet, adjusted EBIT margin is up YoY because of operational efficiency measures that have been made for the group.

Growth

Humble Group is up TTM every quarter since Q1 2023, shows continuous growth. Humble Group's companies continue to produce new healthier products with unique features that do not compromise the taste of the products. The Nordic distributions have a wide range of different sectors that they distribute for, from household items, Scandinavian foods, European and Asian foods, this makes Humble diversified and less cyclical. Quality nutritions are having a break in growth, but the overall trend is there, and I believe more quality nutritions and health snacks is only getting popular and popular.

Sustainable care has continued momentum, and is another trend where consumers are looking for better and premium personal care products, and they are willing to pay a smaller premium for better solutions and products.

However, I believe Humble group's main growth will come from their future snacking segment. This segment does not only have the highest margins, but they also have the strongest growth by far. In this segment, they have managed to produce new, interesting products that consumers love. These products also have a higher price than their counterparts. Pandy candy for examples sell for almost twice the price per kilo as ordinary candy sold in Norway and Sweden. However, they have been smart with their packaging only selling 25 gram bags, so it seems cheap, and for some consumer that is just enough candy for a day.

If future snacking continues to grow double-digit the next five years, then I believe future snacking can be their biggest segment and also increase the group's EBIT margins closer to 6% since future snacking current EBIT margin is 8.5%. The current EBIT TTM 2025 is SEK 391 million. However, I believe future snacking itself will be able to achieve more than SEK 272 million EBIT by 2030. If everything else stays the same, we could expect an EPS of SEK 0.19. That is more than half of the current EPS of SEK 0.26, which shows the potential Humble group has and especially within this segment.

Risks

One of the reasons Humble Group has managed to grow so fast the past years is due to their big IPO in 2021 and that they also leveraged up at the same time. In 2021, their debt to equity ratio stood at 51%, but now has decreased down to 30%.

The existing credit facility agreement includes terms and conditions implying that the Net Interest Bearing Debt in relation to LTM Adjusted EBITDA Proforma excluding leasing must not exceed 3.25x and that the LTM Adjusted EBITDA in relation to Net Financial Expenses (as defined in the credit facility agreement) shall not be less than 4.00x at the end of this period. These terms and conditions have been met since the credit facility agreement was entered in the second quarter 2023.

We also intend to reduce our interest expenses going forward through quarterly amortisation of bank loans and increased profitability in absolute terms, where a reduced level of debt relative to EBITDA is expected to contribute to a lower financing cost for the Group.

Their current EBITDA to Net debt ratio is 2.8x, but their goal is to come closer to 2.5x because that will help reduce financing costs for the group.

I believe lower growth is Humble group's biggest challenge. If they don't manage to increase both profitability and top-line, then this is not the place to be invested.

Valuation and momentum

I have made a discounted EPS valuation based on three different scenarios to calculate the share price. All scenario's use the same EPS, which is SEK 0.4, and a discount rate of 10% which is my required rate of return and which I use for all my valuations.

The first scenario is the normal case, and it is based on an 8% growth rate and terminal multiple of 30x, which is today's current PE ratio. Calculates a share price of SEK 13.6 or $1.36.

The second scenario is the best case, it is based on a 10% growth rate, and a terminal multiple of 35x which is based on pre-historic valuations. Calculates a share price of 18 SEK, or $1.8 per share.

The third scenario is the worst case, it is based on a 3% growth rate, and a terminal multiple of 20x. This scenario shows how far down Humble group can go if their growth slows down. It calculates a share price of SEK 7 or $0.7.

Humble Group momentum not pointing in the right direction, the main ticker is down 33% on the Nasdaq Stockholm. The 1-month is up by a small amount of 3%, and the 3-month is still down considerably to -13%. This is definitely not a good time to invest.

Conclusion

Humble Group has had exceptional growth the past few years, but they have reached a more stable growth now. Some segments have struggled with either less growth or declining growth. However, the company is still growing 4% YoY, and I believe they will witness more growth going forward from their future snacking segment. This segment alone grew 19% YoY, and their ability to keep innovating and create interesting products is the reason they will keep growing. I believe this segment will be their biggest segment within some years, also increasing overall profitability for the group. Their other segments are witnessing slower growth, but the overall trend is pointing in the right direction. However, Humble Group's current momentum is bad due to slower overall growth and weaker EPS YoY, which is the reason I rate this stock a HOLD, but if I see momentum switching I will be quick to buy this stock whenever this happens!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Heritage Insurance Finds Opportunity In Dislocation
data.description:
In a market where many regional insurers have been wiped out by hurricane damage, fraudulent claims, and rising reinsurance costs, Heritage Insurance (NYSE:HRTG) is back. The company doubled its net income to $30.5 million, or $0.99 per share, in the first quarter of 2025, despite absorbing more than $30 million in catastrophic losses. The stock, which had previously sunk below $10, is now trading around $22. Investors have regained confidence, and for good reason.

The stock’s 140% jump in 12 months begs the question: is it too late to get in, or is this just the beginning? In this analysis, I look at Heritage’s fundamental outlook for the next 1-3 years. I analyze whether the company has become structurally stronger, whether the stock is fairly valued, and whether the risks are proportionate to the expected returns.

Florida Recovery and Structural Advantage

The Florida insurance market has been in crisis for the past few years, with insurers pulling out or going bankrupt in droves, largely due to a combination of hurricane damage and abusive legal structures. But recent legislation limiting claims and cracking down on fraudulent assignment of benefits contracts has changed the dynamics. Heritage sensed the moment well. It temporarily pulled out of high-risk regions, raised its rates, improved its models, and now (with nearly 75% of its distribution open again) has begun to grow in a controlled manner. That gives the company a unique timing advantage: While competitors are still restructuring or holding back, Heritage is already writing policies in markets that have been closed for a long time. Competition has also thinned out, keeping premiums at healthy levels. And with the recent relaxation in reinsurance markets and the fact that the company is already rate-competent in more than 90% of its regions, Heritage is poised to grow its book without undermining margins.

Disasters, Profitability and Confidence

That Heritage managed to hold its ground during a quarter with severe wildfires in California speaks volumes. While other insurers usually write red figures during such disasters, Heritage managed to reduce its combined ratio to 84.5%, compared to 94% a year earlier. The loss ratio even improved by 7.2 percentage points despite higher catastrophe losses, purely due to lower attritional claims and higher net earned premiums. The expense ratio also decreased, partly due to a higher reinsurance commission.

Remarkably, this is already the third quarter in a row in which Heritage has been hit by disasters but has remained profitable. That speaks volumes about the resilience of the business model. With a YoY net profit growth of 115%, the company shows that its recovery strategy is not only working but also scalable. The book value per share increased to $10.62, confirming confidence in the balance sheet. The return on equity came to 39%, a level that is rare for a non-life insurer.

E&S Market and Technological Edge

In addition to the traditional home insurance market, Heritage is also active in the fast-growing ‘Excess & Surplus’ market. Here, insurers can charge more flexible, higher rates in high-risk regions outside of standard regulation. The departure of players such as State Farm and Allstate in states such as California and Louisiana frees up market share that Heritage can fill (under strict underwriting).

What sets Heritage apart here is its technological infrastructure. Through smart partnerships (including with InsurTech Slide and rate model builder Akur8) the company is able to better assess risks and respond more quickly to changing circumstances. The implementation of AI in pricing, fast claims processing after storms, and real-time risk monitoring via satellite data: it all contributes to lower costs and faster decisions. In my opinion, that gives Heritage a clear advantage over conventional players.

Valuation vs. Competitors

Is all this priced in? Not quite. Although the stock has risen sharply, Heritage is trading at a P/E ratio of around 7x based on this year’s expected earnings ($3.11 per share). That’s considerably lower than Palomar Holdings, a competitor that also specializes in catastrophe-prone markets, which trades at over 22x times earnings. James River Group, an E&S player in liability insurance, is having a tougher time and is trading at around 99.5% combined ratio, just at break-even. Heritage shows that profitability is also possible in property-catastrophe lines, provided the underwriting is sharp enough. The P/B multiple is now around 2.0x, which is relatively high for a regional insurer. Still, that’s defensible with an ROE of 39%. The market is starting to treat Heritage as a growth stock again, rather than a turnaround story. In my opinion, that is fair enough because the fundamentals are stronger than they have been in years.

Dividend On Hold But Room For Value Creation

Those looking for an attractive dividend yield will be disappointed for the time being. Heritage has suspended the dividend since 2022 to strengthen the balance sheet. Although equity has recovered significantly, management is prioritising growth and caution. Personally, I think that is wise. Better sustainable profit growth and a solid capital buffer than a dividend resumed too early that will be cancelled at the first disaster.

If Heritage maintains its profitability, a modest dividend reintroduction is plausible in the long term. But until then, shareholder value lies mainly in share price appreciation, not in payouts. For investors looking for immediate income, larger players such as Allianz or Zurich are probably more suitable. Those aiming for value growth will find a rare combination of return and cyclical recovery in Heritage.

Outlook and Risks

The outlook looks positive. The company is steadily opening new sales channels, targeting premium growth in the second half of 2025, and is also seeing its investment income pick up after years of low-interest rates. The average duration of the fixed income portfolio is 3.1 years, which is ideal to benefit from the interest rate environment. Analysts are raising their earnings expectations for 2026 to $3.50 per share, which implies that the stock is currently trading at 6.4x times forward earnings. That is low, even with the risk of catastrophes in mind.

However, risks remain. Hurricanes and other natural disasters are unpredictable and can wipe out all profits in a single quarter. Reinsurance costs can spike in global catastrophe scenarios. Regulatory risks are also real: if Florida relaxes the current rules, the loss burden could increase again. And expanding too quickly without discipline could repeat old mistakes.

However, I see these risks as manageable. Heritage has learned from the past and has shown discipline in every quarter since the reform. Costs are under control, reinsurance is solid, and technology makes risk more transparent than ever. For investors willing to keep an eye on the barometer, Heritage Insurance offers a promising risk-return ratio.

Conclusion

I give Heritage Insurance a “Buy-Rating”. After the rally, the stock is no longer undervalued, but it is also not yet expensive. The market does not seem to be fully convinced of the sustainability of the earnings growth. If Heritage continues to be profitable in the coming quarters, that sentiment could change.

If current trends continue, I see room for the stock to grow to $28–$30 within 12 to 18 months. At $22, the stock trades at just 6.3x forward 2025 earnings, assuming EPS of $3.50. If the company maintains sub-90% combined ratios and mid-single-digit premium growth, a fair valuation of 8x–9x forward earnings would not be aggressive. Applying that to expected earnings of $3.50–$4.00 per share implies a range between $28 and $36, with my base case target leaning conservative due to potential catastrophe volatility. This scenario does not rely on multiple expansion beyond mid-cycle sector norms and assumes no dividend reinstatement. Should either occur, the upside could exceed $30 even in a neutral market.

For investors with a 1- to 3-year horizon, this is an opportunity to get into a reborn insurer that seems to have weathered its storms. As long as the company stays on course and the weather cooperates, HRTG offers above-average potential, with above-average risk. As always with catastrophe insurers: keep an eye on the weather forecast but enjoy the premium as long as things remain calm.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 06/21/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: SPXS: The Leveraged ETF That Could Make Or Break Your Portfolio
data.description:
Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bear 3x Shares (NYSEARCA:SPXS) is a leveraged ETF designed to provide traders with 3x the daily inverse performance of the S&P 500 (SPX).

With increasing uncertainty in the market, particularly with geopolitical risks rising, I believe there may be more aggressive, volatile movements in the market that may provide traders an opportunity to capitalize with added risk.

The current geopolitical landscape is becoming more volatile as the Ukrainian & Russian war intensifies, strikes between Iran and Israel become more severe, Chinese activity in the South China Sea raises concerns in Taiwan, and President Trump weighs stepping in to assist Israel in Iranian attacks; there may be significant risks to consider relating to the global markets, including the technology supply chains and energy security, amongst other factors. This level of uncertainty and risk may violently whiplash the market, creating opportunities for traders to benefit from both the upside & downside movements.

Under the Direxion umbrella, traders may consider (SPXL) for bullish market moves and SPXS for bearish moves. In this report, we’ll discuss the mechanics behind SPXS, the 3x inverse ETF.

SPXS is a leveraged strategy that targets 3x the daily return of the underlying index, the S&P 500. SPXS effectively achieves this target with the use of index swaps as a payer in which SPXS is short the underlying index. In so doing, SPXS holds a -300% short position in swaps.

Because of how swaps are designed, the basis is reset daily, meaning that performance will pertain strictly to the day-to-day performance of the swap position. This is reflected in SPXS in which the ETF strategy will only achieve the 3x inverse daily return of the S&P 500 during a single trading day. During periods of heightened volatility, the actual return may deviate from the target return.

As a result of the daily reset, holding a position for more than a single day may lead to a compounding effect on performance when compared to the underlying index’s performance, leading to amplified performance. During periods of consecutive days of market declines, SPXS can realize an amplified return substantially higher than the 3x target.

With leveraged strategies comes substantial downside risk if not used appropriately. Multiple days of market growth can lead to substantial losses in SPXS, and in certain cases, significant value decay.

Given the high trading volumes of SPXS, value decay isn’t as prevalent to the downside as experienced in other leveraged strategies. Accordingly, SPXS has a current trading volume of 59mm shares trading hands daily. This compares to SPXL with a trading volume of 2.7mm shares.

Though I wouldn’t consider this factor as protection to downside risk, I believe that it offers a certain degree of protection from compounding skew from the 3x target.

Given the general long-term growth nature of the market, SPXS should not be considered a long-term strategy. Overall, I only recommend it to be utilized for daily performance alone, as investors may realize amplified losses as a result of the leveraged performance.

Given the nature of leveraged strategies, I believe a trader must take the steps in creating a disciplined trading strategy prior to entering into a position. More importantly, a trader must portray strict sell discipline to ensure losses accrued do not impact their overall portfolio if a trade does not work out as intended.

In terms of performance expectations, exogenous macroeconomic changes will likely impact SPXS in the near-term. As stated above, geopolitical risk could create substantial volatility in the markets, especially if the US gets more involved in either conflict.

One of the most impactful risks related to this could be a conflict between China and Taiwan, impacting a substantial proportion of top constituents of the S&P 500 for semiconductor fabrication and sourcing. With the presumption that growth across the hyperscalers is dependent on sourcing Nvidia (NVDA) GPUs to increase cloud compute capacity for customers, a disruption to the supply chain could have a severe impact on the market.

Other Risks to Consider Before

There are inherent risks in investing in leveraged strategies. Because these strategies are meant to achieve their stated multiple over the underlying index on a daily basis, holding these strategies beyond a single day can lead to skewed performance, especially following consecutive days of upside or downside risk. This can lead to a compounding effect as well as value decay as a result of the daily reset in the derivatives positions.

Leveraged strategies also add an extra layer of risk to amplified downside potential. For example, if the underlying index were to appreciate by 2% in a single day, a 3x strategy would appreciate by 6%, meaning that an investment of $100 would increase to $106. If the following day results in a -2% decline in the underlying index, or a -6% decline in the leveraged strategy, the investment will decline to $99.64.

Holding leveraged strategies for longer than a single day can lead to a compounding effect on one’s position, potentially leading to significant skews from the intended multiplier. During multiple days of downside risk, an investor can experience significant value decay, potentially resulting in the inability to recover losses.

Liquidity must be considered prior to investing in a leveraged fund strategy. Given that these funds are designed to be traded daily, trading volumes must be considered to ensure that cost-in/cost-out isn’t deteriorated by spreads.

You can review additional risks here.

Before making a trading or investment decision in leveraged strategies, be sure to fully understand your risk profile and set a trading mandate to manage these risks. I believe preemptively setting parameters for selling out of a position is prudent in ensuring that an investor appropriately manages downside risk.

Overall, I believe SPXS can provide traders with an enhanced, leveraged, inverse performance to the S&P 500 when used appropriately. This strategy portrays substantial liquidity, allowing for traders to enter and exit positions without undertaking substantial spread costs. Given the general direction of the market, I am rating SPXS with a HOLD rating.

data.title: SDY: Dividend Stocks Give Way To The Mag 7 In Q2 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Global stocks are hiking their dividends by a sturdy amount so far in the second quarter. I called out this trend in a recent Caterpillar (CAT) article. Today, I’d like to home in on the SPDR S&P Dividend ETF (NYSEARCA:SDY). I was upbeat on the fund last summer, expecting relative strength to persist through the end of 2025, but its alpha has wavered over recent months.

Moreover, the valuation situation is roughly the same as was seen in August 2024. To quote the previous article, “The current price-to-earnings ratio is not a big bargain at close to 19 times.” The same is true today.

With a similar price tag and a less-impressive technical situation, I am downgrading SDY to a hold. Shares have merely been flat since my August 2024 analysis.

SDY Sports Soft Relative Strength Since Last Summer

According to the issuer, SDY offers investors exposure to the highest-yielding stocks in the S&P Composite 1500 Index via tracking the S&P High Yield Dividend Aristocrats Index. The fund follows a passively managed approach whereby it owns shares of firms that have a history of increasing dividends every year for at least 20 consecutive years. Stocks within the Index are weighted by indicated yield.

SDY is a large ETF with $19.7 billion in assets under management as of June 18, 2025. That’s down from $20.8 billion at the time of my previous assessment. Its annual expense ratio is somewhat low at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 2.61%--about twice that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has turned south, however, as indicated by its soft C- ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. SDY is also a risky portfolio, though annualized standard deviation metrics may be pushed upward due to the high volatility seen in April. Finally, liquidity is healthy—average daily volume is more than 275,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is just three basis points, per SSGA Funds.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box, but there’s a clear focus on value equities. Just 5% of the allocation is classified as growth. Prospective investors should also be aware that most of the ETF’s assets are considered SMID cap, which can add to the risk profile.

Today, the P/E ratio is a bit above 18x, close to the valuation from 10 months ago. While that’s still cheaper than the S&P 500’s earnings multiple, it’s not an outright bargain. Framed against a long-term earnings growth rate of 7.5%, the PEG ratio is lofty, above two.

SDY: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the allocation, Industrials, which is the best-performing S&P 500 sector so far this year, is the largest weighting in SDY. That hasn’t been all that much of a boon, though, as SDY is up by just 2% in 2025, including dividends.

Information Technology is a notable underweight. It’s just 9.6% of SDY, compared to a 34% position in the S&P 500. The Mag 7 stocks have been on an alpha heater over the past two months, leading to some negative relative performance with SDY.

SDY: Holdings & Dividend Information

YTD Performance View: Mag 7 ETF Soaring Since Late April, SDY Alpha Sags

There’s good news here, however. July has been SDY’s best month over the past 10 years in terms of the positivity rate. Up in each of the last 10 Julys, we often see strong performance numbers to kick off the second half. Of course, August and September are notoriously volatile.

SDY: Bullish July Trends

The Technical Take

With an “uncheap” valuation and bullish seasonal trends, SDY’s technical situation is not ideal. Notice in the chart below that shares remain under a key downtrend resistance line off the all-time high late last year. What’s more, the long-term 200-day moving average has turned flat in its slope, suggesting that there’s an ongoing battle between the bulls and bears. Hence, the share price is at a key spot that I’ve noted on that chart—near the $136 point.

If we see an upside breakout above, say, $138, then the 2024 highs would certainly be achievable in 2H. I also like that there’s now a high amount of volume by price under today’s level. But take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph—it is ominously ranging in a bearish zone between 20 and 60. I’d like to see improved RSI trends to complement a would-be breakout in price. For now, support is near $132.

SDY: A Confluence of Resistance, Soft RSI Trends, Flat 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on SDY. This yield-weighted dividend aristocrat ETF performed very well from November 2023 through late October, but its absolute and relative strength have petered out. The current P/E doesn’t scream cheap, and technicals are questionable.

data.title: MBS: High-Quality Income ETF, 5.1% Dividend Yield
data.description:
The Angel Oak Mortgage-Backed Securities ETF (NASDAQ:MBS) is an actively managed MBS ETF. It offers investors a high-quality portfolio, an above-average 5.1% dividend yield, and a good performance track-record. It is an expensive ETF, with a 0.80% expense ratio. I settled on a buy rating, as I believe the fund's advantages outweigh its disadvantages, but it was a close call.

MBS - Overview and Analysis

Strategy and Portfolio

MBS is an actively managed ETF investing in MBS, leaning agency, but with significant non-agency investments.

As per MBS's documentation, Angel Oak, the manager, focuses on structured credit / mortgages, and so seems well-poised for managing the fund. Insofar as the fund's management team is able to select the right investments and implement the right investment / trading strategies, the fund should generate alpha and outperform its peers. CARY is managed by Angel Oak too, focuses on MBS and has an outstanding track-record, so there does seem to be some truth to this.

MBS is not a particularly well-diversified fund, with investments in only 153 securities, and with agency MBS / Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac accounting for over half of the fund's portfolio. As these are high-quality holdings, risks are not particularly high, nor are significant losses from individual defaults all that likely. MBS is significantly exposed to conditions in the mortgage market, though, something of a risk factor.

As a comparison, CARY has sizable investments in CLOs and similar assets, which would perform particularly well during a period of rising rates. MBS does not, so is more exposed to rates, likelier to see significant losses if these decrease. Balanced bond portfolios might perform adequately regardless of macro conditions, but that is most definitely not the case for MBS.

Risk and Volatility

MBS's credit risk is quite low, with the fund focusing on AAA investments, and by quite a large margin.

MBS's high-quality portfolio benefits investors in two key ways.

First, high-quality means low default rates, which minimizes long-term capital losses. MBS's credit quality is such that share prices should remain stable long-term, losses, if any, should be quite low, below single-digits. With inception in early 2024, the fund does not have a sufficiently long-term track-record for us to analyze these issues. In the interest of full disclosure, share prices are slightly higher since inception, a bit better than expected.

The second benefit of MBS's high-quality portfolio, is that high-quality fixed-income securities tend to increase in price during downturns and recessions, due to Federal Reserve policy and a general flight-to-quality effect. The fund has yet to experience a recession, but this phenomenon is well-known and should benefit MBS too. The iShares MBS ETF (MBB) focuses on the same securities as MBS, and saw small gains during the pandemic. Gains were much lower than those of treasuries, but there were gains, unlike riskier high-yield bonds or equities.

MBS's duration of 6.1 years is about average, with most of the larger bond ETFs having durations of around 6 years. Expect about average sensitivity to rates, a moderate number of gains when rates decrease, moderate number of losses when rates increase. MBS did not experience much of the prior Fed hiking cycle, the best example of this dynamic. Treasury rates did decline during the first months of MBS's existence, during which share prices increased, as expected.

MBS should be a relatively safe, low-volatility ETF, considering its high-quality, medium-duration portfolio. Volatility should spike during periods of significant rate volatility, but these are somewhat rare, 2022 to 2024 notwithstanding. Volatility shouldn't be too different from that of the average / the largest bond ETF, as most of these focus on treasuries, MBS, and similar securities. MBS has had slightly / moderate below-average volatility compared to peers, doing a bit better in this regard than expected.

MBS is a bit more volatile than some of my favorite income ETFs, including CARY and the iShares Flexible Income Active ETF (BINC). MBS is a reasonably safe, low-volatility choice, but there are better ones in this regard out there.

Dividend Analysis

MBS's 5.1% dividend yield is reasonably good, slightly higher than that of most bonds and bond sub-asset classes, somewhat lower than riskier high-yield bonds and similar investments. Risk-adjusted yields are stronger, accounting for the fund's high-quality portfolio. The fund's yield is 1.0% higher than that of MBS benchmarks, which seems like the most relevant comparison.

MBS's moderate duration means that the fund's dividends should not be immediately impacted by changes in Federal Reserve policy or market conditions.

Most of my top income ETFs have higher yields than MBS, including CARY and BINC:

Both of those have lower durations, too, so are more swiftly impacted by Fed cuts. Sufficient of these would cause their yields to drop below those of MBS, for a time, at least.

Performance Track-Record

MBS has slightly outperformed most bonds and bond sub-asset classes since inception, with the exception of high-yield bonds and other riskier types of investments. I'm seeing no downsides here, or periods of significant, even moderate, sustained underperformance. Most importantly, the fund has outperformed mortgage-backed security benchmark ETFs, although only very slightly so.

My favorite income ETFs have had marginally higher returns, with quite a bit less volatility. Duration does not explain these results, as these have outperformed YTD, even as rates trend downwards (although not by a lot).

Overall Assessment

MBS's most significant negatives are its 0.80% expense ratio, and that it compares somewhat unfavorably to some of its peers (my top income ETFs). It has a higher duration too, which is a net negative long-term, but could easily be a short-term positive (just have the Fed cut rates).

It does have several important advantages, including an above-average dividend yield, performance track-record, and high-quality portfolio. My top income ETFs have the first two, but have materially lower credit quality (still good, but definitely not as good as MBS).

At first, I leaned towards a hold rating, as that expense ratio seemed excessive. Settled on a buy as it does compare favorably to most other bond sub-asset classes and does not seem that much worse than CARY or BINC. Still prefer the latter two, but MBS is a reasonable choice too.

Conclusion

MBS's high-quality portfolio, good 5.1% dividend yield and performance track-record, make the fund a buy.

data.title: Exor: Positive Portfolio Changes Support Continued Buy Rating
data.description:
Introduction

In late 2024, I reviewed Exor N.V. (OTCPK:EXXRF) and landed on a Buy rating, based largely on my expectation that the stock’s wide gap to its net asset value (‘NAV’) would eventually close. The word ‘eventually’ means different things to different investors, so to avoid confusion, my thinking was that the NAV discount closure scenario would play out over a number of years. The other aspect of Exor that supported my Buy rating was my view that the group’s underlying holdings were capable of delivering satisfactory medium-term NAV growth.

In this note, I’ll update readers on significant changes that Exor has made since my December 2024 publication, and I will consider whether or not the conclusions that resulted in my previous Buy rating remain valid.

FY24 NAV Growth

In absolute terms, Exor’s NAV per share growth of 9% in FY24 is perfectly respectable, and I’d be more than happy if the group could generate that type of return on a sustainable basis. However, relative to Exor’s goal to outperform the MSCI World Index (denominated in EUR), FY24 was disappointing, with underperformance of almost -16%. Despite the poor FY24 outcome, Exor’s longer-term performance remains strong, with average returns 6% pa ahead of the index.

Exhibit 1:

It is worth noting the impact of Ferrari (RACE) on Exor’s FY24 NAV growth. Ferrari is Exor’s largest holding. As shown in Exhibit 2, without Ferrari’s contribution, Exor’s NAV per share would have fallen by about -4.8% in FY24.

It will be no surprise to anyone who has been following the fortunes of Stellantis (STLA) that Exor’s FY24 returns were impacted badly by its holding in the troubled auto group. STLA is the second largest exposure in the portfolio, and I flagged this holding as a downside risk factor for Exor in my previous note. Unfortunately, after STLA’s total shareholder return of -36% in FY24, this investment has continued to put downward pressure on Exor’s NAV in FY25.

Exhibit 2:

Listed Holdings – Multiple Leadership Changes

I think it is fair to say that STLA has been struggling due to a combination of external and internal factors. Poor operational execution ultimately resulted in the resignation of CEO Carlos Tavares in December 2024. The process to appoint the replacement for Tavares has been rather slow, and STLA has only recently announced that Antonio Filosa, who has been with the group for over two decades, will find himself in the hot seat from late June 2025. Filosa faces a difficult challenge, and it seems likely that a turnaround for STLA will take some time.

Two of Exor’s other listed holdings had CEO changes in FY24 – CNH Industrial (9.4% of FY24 GAV) and Iveco (1.6% of FY24 GAV). News broke in April 2024 that CNH Industrial CEO Scott Wine was departing to ‘pursue other interests’ after only three years in the job. Gerrit Marx was poached from Iveco, and appointed to the role of CNH Industrial CEO effective July 1, 2024.

Marx had joined CNH Industrial in 2019, as President of Commercial and Specialty Vehicles. He subsequently became CEO of Iveco when it was spun off from CNH Industrial in early 2022. Following the departure of Marx, Iveco announced that Olof Persson, an Iveco Group Board Independent Director, would take over as CEO. It is somewhat unusual for a board member to move into a CEO role, and often when this occurs the appointment is temporary in nature. However, Olof Persson remains CEO at present. I note that Persson is currently Chairman of the Board of New Wave Group AB, a Swedish company listed on the Nasdaq Stockholm Large Cap Index. I would question Persson’s ability to adequately handle having a Chairman role at the same time as being the CEO of Iveco; this must be seen as a genuine corporate governance concern for Iveco investors.

CEO exits are nearly always disruptive, with the new arrival often triggering a host of senior executive team changes. A quick review of news announcements for both CNH Industrial and Iveco reveals that both companies have experienced a lot of turnover of senior executives since the appointment of their new CEOs. Major strategic shifts are also quite likely to occur once the new CEO has settled in. With three of Exor’s seven listed holdings (collectively making up ~24% of Exor GAV) having new CEOs, the group’s portfolio is not as operationally stable as I would like it to be.

Significant Changes In FY25

I have previously written about my concern regarding the size of Exor’s exposure to Ferrari. This concern reflected single-name concentration risk, and was not based on any particular worry regarding Ferrari. As at FY24, Ferrari represented ~43.2% of gross asset value (‘GAV’), up from ~39.3% as at 1H24.

I was therefore pleased to see Exor make a strategic decision to reduce its holding in Ferrari in late February 2025. The sale released ~EUR 3 billion of capital, with Exor noting that the transaction both reduced portfolio concentration and provided the funds for sizable new acquisitions. The sell-down has clearly not eliminated the Ferrari concentration downside risk for Exor, but it is a step in the right direction.

The second major change over recent months was the completion of an EUR 1 billion buyback tender offer in late April 2025 at a price of EUR 81.6027 per share. With Exor’s stock trading at a large discount to NAV, repurchasing ~5.5% of the group’s outstanding shares strikes me as a sensible investment by Exor.

The final material change worth highlighting is that Exor has further increased its holding in Philips (PHG) in FY25. As at FY24, Exor owned ~17.5% of Philips. Additional share purchases in FY25 had taken Exor’s ownership stake to ~18.7% as at March 27, 2025. I am supportive of increased exposure to Philips, as this will tend to reduce Exor’s overall exposure to cyclical industries (which I estimate to be around 50% measured at the GAV level).

NAV Discount Update

As shown in Exhibit 3, Exor’s NAV per share as of FY24 was EUR 178.78. With Exor currently trading at EUR 81.90, the implied NAV discount (relative to the FY24 NAV) is ~54%. However, this is not the right metric to look at, as it ignores changes in the values of Exor’s assets post December 31, 2024 and the material portfolio changes referenced above.

Exhibit 3:

In Exhibit 4 I set out my estimate of the current NAV per share for Exor. This estimate captures changes in the share prices of Exor’s listed holdings since FY24, adjusts for items (which are relatively small in the scheme of things) such as net finance expenses and general/administrative expenses, and also takes into account the portfolio changes that I have highlighted in this note. Consistent with my previous analysis, I have valued Exor’s venture assets at 80% of the most recently reported book value; the haircut of 20% reflects my view that Exor’s balance sheet valuation of the venture assets might be optimistic.

Exhibit 4:

The final adjustment made in the calculations shown in Exhibit 4 is to deduct the capitalized value of future general/administrative expenses from the base NAV. As Exor’s general/administrative expenses are low relative to the group’s asset base, this adjustment does not actually make much of a difference. However, I include the calculations in order to provide what I deem to be the ‘best’ measure of adjusted NAV.

The analysis above points to Exor currently trading at a discount to NAV of around 52%. This discount level is slightly wider than I calculated back in late December 2024. Exhibit 5 shows Exor’s calculation of the stock’s NAV discount over time. The current discount level is much higher than the since inception average of ~30%. From late 2013 until the pandemic, Exor appears to have traded at an average NAV discount of ~20%.

Exhibit 5:

Closing Comments & Rating Update

Relative to my previous review of Exor, my confidence in the stock’s investment case has been strengthened by the group’s decisions to reduce Ferrari’s portfolio concentration and execute a large buyback. I also regard the increased portfolio exposure to Philips as a positive.

However, it’s not all good news. Leadership instability within three of Exor’s largest companies is concerning, and a ~35% fall in the Stellantis share price since FY24 are notable negatives. Further, I would argue that relative to late December 2024, the risk-adjusted outlook for global economic growth has deteriorated, which represents a challenge for Exor’s high exposure to cyclical industries.

On balance, I remain reasonably comfortable with the group’s asset profile, and continue to hold the view that Exor can deliver satisfactory NAV growth over time. Further, I see no reason to step away from my assumption that Exor’s NAV discount can close to around the 30% level over time, and on that basis, the NAV discount closure scenario provides potential upside of close to +50%.

Having completed this review, as things stand today, I do not see a compelling argument to step away from a Buy rating on Exor. I remind readers that my analysis and conclusions are based on medium-term thinking, and that the NAV discount closure scenario will most likely play out over a number of years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Fidelity National: Elevated Rates Leave Shares Unattractive
data.description:
While shares of Fidelity National Financial (NYSE:FNF) have been a solid performer over the past year, adding 10%, the stock has fallen over 15% from its high, amid elevated rates and disappointing performance at its publicly traded subsidiary F&G (FG). I last covered Fidelity National in November 2024 when I rated the stock a “sell,” given my expectation that mortgage origination volumes would remain low due to elevated rates. That has largely played out, and the stock has lost 11% while the market is flat. Given this underperformance, now is a good time to see if shares are sufficiently pricing in the macro difficulties. I remain cautious.

FNF is a leading title insurance player, and its business is highly sensitive to mortgage activity

Fidelity National is the nation’s leading title insurance company. Fidelity has a 32% market share in title insurance and is a top two or three players in nearly every state, as you can see below. Given its leading size, its results are primarily impacted by shifts in origination volume, as potential market share gains are limited. Generally speaking, anytime you take out a mortgage, to buy or refinance a home, you need to buy title insurance.

As such, volumes and profits are highly dependent on housing market activity and mortgage rates. Generally speaking, this type of insurance it extremely low-loss, given excellent property-level record keeping in the United States. While not a no-loss, business, loss rates are typically in the 3-4% range, extremely low. Accordingly, volumes drive profits far more than variations in underwriting.

Over the past few years, as mortgage rates have risen and housing market activity has slowed, FNF revenues and margins have faced significant pressures. As you can see, FNF revenue last year was down about 33% from its highs. 2021 was helped by low rates, causing a surge in refinancing beyond home purchase demand. With mortgage rates stubbornly above 6.5%, refinancing opportunity is limited, and I generally believe that mortgage rates need to be closer to 6% to unlock meaningful refinancing activity.

FNF's stake in F&G is worth ~$13/share

Beyond its core title business, Fidelity owns about 82% of F&G, even participating in F&G’s March equity issuance, given its desire to maintain an 80%+ stake. I do not expect a shift in this desire over the near or medium term. This stake is worth about $3.5 billion, or about $13 per share. I prefer to value FNF on a standalone basis to determine the value of its title business and then add the market value of its FG stake, rather than focus on consolidated results.

I have covered F&G separately, and you can read my latest article from May here. Given weak results and questionable equity issuance, I believe F&G management, and by extension, FNF, faces significant credibility challenges, which will weigh on valuation for some time. I have maintained a “buy” given how much valuation has been compressed, but enthusiasm for shares has been dampened given missteps over the past six months.

Recent results were mixed as weak residential activity offsets a commercial recovery

Looking at recent results, in the company’s first quarter, Fidelity earned $0.78, which missed estimates by $0.33. Title earnings rose to $158 million from $130 million a year ago, as the company benefited from a somewhat better environment, even as activity levels remain muted overall. It had 343,000 opened orders from 315,000 last year, and refinances were 25% of activity from 21% year ago. As a result, title premiums were $1.72 billion, up $200 million from last year. Meanwhile, FNF had $672 million of personnel costs, up a more modest 9% from last year, supporting margin expansion of 100bps to 11.7%.

As you can see below, mortgage rates dipped a bit late last year. This helped to create a modest burst of refinancing activity in Q4 and into Q1, as I had suggested might occur last November. However, since then, rates have moved back above 6.75%, which will likely choke off this nascent recovery. With elevated long-term yields, a patient Fed given potential tariff inflation risk, and Republicans likely to pass a tax cut bill that will increase debt issuance, I struggle to see a meaningful decline in mortgage rates over the next six months, which is likely to keep activity subdued.

Indeed, on the earnings call, management noted that while April was up 3% from Q1, it was down 3% from last year, which will be a headwind for Q2 results. One tailwind has been higher premiums. The average residential fee per file was up 6% from last year at $3,761. I expect this to remain elevated, as strained affordability will likely keep down payments low and mortgage balances high. However, if we see a more meaningful downturn in home prices (which is not my base case), we could see fee per file begin to come down.

One area of genuine strength was on the commercial side. FNF generated $293 million of commercial revenue, up from $238 million a year ago. Beyond solid volume growth, the average fee per file was up 15% to $11,300. After years of inactivity, the commercial market is beginning to open up again, and I do expect this better level of transaction activity to persist, even in this elevated rate environment.

FNF has a high-quality investment portfolio and a solid balance sheet

As with any insurer, FNF also maintains an investment portfolio, on which it earns interest. FNF has a very high-quality portfolio, and it has benefited from reinvesting at higher rates. It generated $83 million of investment income, flat with last year, even though there was one less calendar day, a 1% headwind. My expectation is for 2 rate cuts by the Fed this year, with risk skewed to fewer cuts than more cuts. In this scenario, we should see FNF generate $85-90 million of investment income a quarter.

FNF generated $187 million of cash from operations, excluding F&G. This covers its 3.7% dividend, which I view as secure. We also saw the company do $25 million of Q1 buybacks after essentially no buybacks in 2023-2024. Management expects to continue buybacks throughout the year. It does have $687 million of holding company cash and investments, which it can use to finance repurchases. I expect about a $50 million quarterly pace going forward. The company currently has 29.2% debt to capital at the high end of its 20-30% operating target, which does limit how much repurchase activity can increase in my view.

With tepid economic activity, elevated rates, and slowing refinance activity, I expect residential title volumes to be flat to down over the balance of 2025 relative to 2024, a reversal from Q1’s gain. While commercial volumes should increase, I would expect total revenues to be flattish or down slightly. With some margin accretion thanks to technology investments, that can support $3.10-$3.30 in EPS, excluding FG.

Interest rates are the primary risk

As with any stock, FNF has risks and opportunities. In its core title business, the primary risk to activity is higher interest rates, which would generally slow the number of housing transactions. Conversely, lower rates of 6% or lower would likely spur a meaningful surge of refinancing activity. The worst-case rate outcome is one where the Fed cuts rates, but long-term interest rates rise (a "steepening" of the yield curve") as this would reduce FNF's investment income while also pressuring mortgage activity. I remain very concerned about the impact of rates on the business.

Aside from interest rates, F&G represents ~25% of FNF's market value. As such, a further decline in FG shares will pressure FNF. I generally view F&G stock as undervalued, so I view this as a manageable risk. Plus, each ~10% move in F&G shares is worth just ~2% to FNF shares, reducing the impact.

Given the strong history of underwriting and solid US property records, I do not see worsening underwriting losses as likely or a material risk. Additionally, with the Trump Administration, regulatory pressure on the title insurance sector appears more modest.

Valuation is unattractive, leaving FNF as a sell.

Removing the value of its FG stake, FNF is trading at ~$42, or about 13.1x earnings. With most insurers trading below 12x, this multiple only really works if investors believe there will be a coming decline in mortgage rates that can cause origination volume to jump. I do not see that as imminent, and this premium to the insurance sector more broadly is unappealing. Indeed, investors can also buy homebuilders for less than 10x, if they believe in a housing market rebound, and likely see more upside than in FNF.

First American Financial, more of a pure play title insurer, trades at about 11x, and while FNF's business is more scaled, I view a ~20% relative premium as excessive, given both businesses have the same macro risk. For investors who want to express a view that the housing market has limited downside, I would also rather own a mortgage insurer like Radian (RDN) at ~9x earnings, and it has more buyback capacity. While higher beta, a company like Toll (TOL) is likely to outperform any of these companies in a substantially lower rate world.

Even after its decline, partly due to a weaker valuation in FG, I see no rush to get into FNF. Results are likely to be flat to last year at best, and as such, I see shares continuing to be dead money. Plus, there are more attractive alternatives for investors who have a more bullish view of housing than I do. I would avoid the stock unless we saw shares drop to $50 or lower, to more fully price in a prolonged period of housing market slowness.
